entry.title: Integrate CAP application as outbound channel for SAP Document AI
entry.content:
The Challenge

When integrating SAP Document AI as a document extraction engine into a broader BTP solution, one of the first hurdles you’ll encounter is the outbound callback mechanism. Document AI supports notifying an external system once extraction is complete — a great feature for building event-driven, asynchronous document processing pipelines.

However, there’s a subtle but important quirk: Document AI unconditionally performs a CSRF token preflight before sending the actual extraction result callback. It fires a GET request with the header X-CSRF-Token: Fetch to your callback endpoint regardless of whether CSRF protection is actually in place on the receiving end.

This behavior cannot be disabled in the Document AI configuration.

The solution? Register Express middleware directly during CAP’s bootstrap phase — before the framework takes over — and handle both the preflight and the callback there.

Architecture Overview

SAP Document AI
      │
      │  ① GET /callback/notify
      │     X-CSRF-Token: Fetch
      │◄─── X-CSRF-Token: <random-token>
      │
      │  ② POST /callback/notify
      │     X-CSRF-Token: <random-token>
      │     Body: notification payload
      ▼
┌──────────────────────────────────────┐
│          CAP Application             │
│                                      │
│  ┌────────────────────────────────┐  │
│  │     csrf-middleware.js         │  │
│  │  • Issues token on GET/HEAD    │  │
│  │  • Validates token on POST     │  │
│  └───────────────┬────────────────┘  │
│                  │                   │
│  ┌───────────────▼────────────────┐  │
│  │     callback-service.js        │  │
│  │  • Parses extraction payload   │  │
│  │  • Matches & finalises job     │  │
│  └────────────────────────────────┘  │
└──────────────────────────────────────┘

The middleware intercepts both requests before CAP’s own routing and CSRF logic runs, acting as a transparent proxy layer between Document AI and your business logic.

Implementation Walkthrough

1. Hooking into the CAP Bootstrap

The key to this entire approach is cds.on('bootstrap', (app) => { ... }). This event fires after CAP has initialized the underlying Express application but before it registers its own middleware stack. This gives us direct, unmediated access to the raw Express app object.

The server.js is deliberately thin. Its only responsibility is wiring the CSRF middleware into the Express stack at the right moment:

'use strict';

const cds = require('@sap/cds');
const csrfMiddleware = require('./csrf-middleware');

cds.on('bootstrap', (app) => {
    app.use(csrfMiddleware);
});

module.exports = cds.server;

⚠️Exporting cds.server is essential — this tells CAP to use the standard server startup while still respecting your bootstrap customizations.

The actual callback business logic lives separately in callback-service.js, keeping concerns cleanly separated. The middleware handles the HTTP-level CSRF handshake; the service handles what to do with the payload.

2. The CSRF Middleware

The full middleware lives in csrf-middleware.js and handles two responsibilities: issuing tokens on preflight requests and validating tokens on incoming POST requests.

Token Lifecycle

Rather than returning a static or meaningless string, the middleware generates a cryptographically random token per preflight, stores it in memory with a TTL, and enforces single-use consumption:

// In-memory token store: token → expiry timestamp
const _tokens = new Map();
const TOKEN_TTL_MS = 10 * 60 * 1000; // 10 minutes

function generateToken() {
    const token = crypto.randomBytes(24).toString('hex');
    _tokens.set(token, Date.now() + TOKEN_TTL_MS);
    return token;
}

function validateToken(token) {
    if (!token) return false;
    const expiry = _tokens.get(token);
    if (!expiry) return false;
    if (Date.now() > expiry) { _tokens.delete(token); return false; }
    _tokens.delete(token); // single-use: consumed on first valid POST
    return true;
}

A periodic cleanup interval ensures expired tokens don’t accumulate indefinitely:

setInterval(() => {
    const now = Date.now();
    for (const [token, expiry] of _tokens.entries()) {
        if (expiry < now) _tokens.delete(token);
    }
}, 60000);

The Middleware Function

The middleware is path-scoped to /callback/* routes and handles each HTTP method appropriately:

function csrfMiddleware(req, res, next) {
    // Only apply to /callback paths
    if (!req.path.startsWith('/callback')) return next();

    const csrfHeader = req.headers['x-csrf-token'];

    // GET or HEAD preflight: issue a fresh token
    if ((req.method === 'GET' || req.method === 'HEAD') 
            && csrfHeader?.toLowerCase() === 'fetch') {
        const token = generateToken();
        log.debug(`CSRF preflight (${req.method}): issued token for ${req.path}`);
        res.set('X-CSRF-Token', token);
        res.set('Allow', 'GET,HEAD,POST');
        res.set('Cache-Control', 'no-cache, no-store');
        return res.status(200).end();
    }

    // POST: validate the token if present
    if (req.method === 'POST') {
        if (csrfHeader && !validateToken(csrfHeader)) {
            log.warn(`CSRF validation failed for POST ${req.path}`);
            return res.status(403).json({ 
                error: { message: 'CSRF token validation failed' } 
            });
        }
        log.debug(`CSRF token accepted for POST ${req.path}`);
    }

    next();
}

3. Handling the Callback Payload

Once the CSRF handshake is complete, Document AI posts the extraction results to /callback/notify. The actual processing logic is registered as a standard CAP service endpoint in callback-service.js.

What Document AI Sends

This is where it gets interesting. SAP Document AI schemas have a toggle that controls how much data is included directly in the callback payload. Depending on your schema configuration, you’ll encounter two patterns:

Option A — Full extraction result in the callback body

When the "include extraction result" toggle is enabled on your Document AI schema, the callback payload contains the complete extraction result directly in the DocumentResult field — serialized as a Python dictionary string representation (not valid JSON). This means you get all extracted field values, confidence scores, and metadata in a single push, with no need to make a follow-up API call to retrieve results.

DocumentResult = "{file_ID=abc-123, extracted_fields=[...], schema=InvoiceSchema, ...}"

⚠️ Note that this is Python repr() format, not JSON. Key-value pairs use = instead of :, and null values appear as None. Your callback handler needs to account for this — either with a dedicated parser or targeted regex extraction for the fields you need.

Option B — Lightweight notification only

When the toggle is disabled (or a simpler integration is preferred), the callback only contains a minimal notification with the NotificationObjectId. You can then use this ID to fetch the structured extraction result on demand via the Document AI DocumentService:

GET /document-service/v1/DocumentService/Documents({NotificationObjectId})/DocumentService.simpleStructure()

The DocumentService.simpleStructure() endpoint returns a clean, normalized JSON representation of the extracted fields — often the more practical choice if you don’t need the full raw extraction metadata and want a consistent, version-stable data contract between Document AI and your CAP application.

Setting Up the Callback Destination

With the CSRF middleware in place, the next step is making the callback URL reachable from SAP Document AI. This involves two things: defining the destination in your mta.yaml so it is automatically provisioned on deployment (ensuring automatic client credential updates), and then registering it as an outbound channel in the Document AI schema configuration UI.

Defining the Destination in mta.yaml

SAP BTP Destination Service allows you to declare destinations declaratively as part of your MTA deployment descriptor. This means the callback destination is created or updated automatically every time you deploy — no manual destination configuration in the BTP cockpit required.

Add a dedicated content deployer module to your mta.yaml:

# ─── Destination content deployer ─────────────────────────────────────────────
- name: docai-cap-dest-content
  type: com.sap.application.content
  requires:
    - name: docai-cap-dest # the name of your destination resource
      parameters:
        content-target: true
    - name: docai-cap-xsuaa # the name of your xsuaa resource
      parameters:
        service-key:
          name: docai-cap-xsuaa-key
    - name: srv-api
  parameters:
    content:
      subaccount:
        existing_destinations_policy: update
        destinations:
          - Name: CAP_DocAI_Callback
            Description: Callback destination for CAP app
            URL: ~{srv-url}/callback/notify
            Authentication: OAuth2ClientCredentials
            TokenServiceInstanceName: docai-cap-xsuaa
            TokenServiceKeyName: docai-cap-xsuaa-key
            HTML5.DynamicDestination: true
  build-parameters:
    no-source: tru

A few things worth calling out:

~{srv-api/srv-url}/callback/notify
The URL is not hardcoded — it is resolved dynamically at deployment time using the srv-api provided parameters of your CAP server module. This means the destination always points to the correct URL regardless of which BTP space or region you deploy to, making the descriptor fully portable across landscapes.

OAuth2ClientCredentials
The callback destination is secured with OAuth2 Client Credentials, backed by the XSUAA instance of your CAP application. When Document AI calls the callback URL, it first fetches a Bearer token from XSUAA using the client credentials stored in docai-cap-scanner-xsuaa-key, and includes it in the Authorization header of every request — both the CSRF preflight GET and the actual callback POST. Your CAP application’s standard XSUAA-based authentication middleware validates this token automatically.

existing_destinations_policy: update
This ensures that redeployments update the destination in place rather than failing on a name conflict. Safe to use in CI/CD pipelines.

HTML5.DynamicDestination: true
Marks the destination as dynamically resolvable, which is required for runtime URL resolution via the destination service.

Configuring the Outbound Channel in SAP Document AI

Once deployed, the destination is available in your BTP subaccount and can be referenced directly from the SAP Document AI configuration UI. Navigate to Channels in Document AI and create an Outbound Channel ensuring the toggle for "Use SAP BTP Destination" is enabled.

 

Once saved, go to your schema, navigate to the Outbound Channel Configuration and add the just configured channel and toggle on/off the inclusion of your extraction results. Specifying the Document Status in which the channel shall be triggered allows you to either immediately forward the notification once the extraction is done (statusReview Needed) or only after a human validation (e.g. status Confirmed) .

With both the destination provisioned automatically via MTA and the outbound channel registered in the Document AI schema UI, the entire integration is reproducible from a single mbt build && cf deploy — no manual post-deployment steps required.

Conclusion

SAP Document AI is a powerful extraction service, but its fixed CSRF preflight behavior creates a real integration challenge when connecting to CAP applications or Integration Flows. By hooking into CAP’s bootstrap lifecycle and registering a focused, well-structured Express middleware, we can elegantly satisfy Document AI’s handshake requirements without patching CAP internals or weakening the application’s overall security posture.

The result is a clean two-layer architecture: the middleware owns the CSRF token lifecycle at the HTTP layer, and the callback service owns the business logic of matching jobs and processing extraction results — each with a single, clear responsibility.

This pattern is also broadly reusable: any time you need to expose a raw HTTP endpoint in a CAP application that bypasses the standard framework middleware (for webhooks, health checks, or third-party callbacks with unusual requirements), cds.on('bootstrap') is your entry point.

Happy building! 🚀

entry.title: Demystifying Model Context Protocol (MCP), Part 2: From Concept to Code
entry.content:
Recap and Next Steps

In Part 1, we laid the foundation for Model Context Protocol. We learnt MCP as an open standard that lets AI applications (hosts) talk to external systems through MCP clients and servers, instead of every team inventing a custom “tool API” for each integration.  We also focused on WHAT, WHY and WHEN of MCP. I strongly recommend going through Part 1 to understand the concepts of MCP and how it helps to streamline the integrations in agentic solutions.

In Part 2, we will focus more on:

  1. Building an MCP server
  2. Exposing tools
  3. Integrating with backend enterprise systems (e-commerce application)
  4. Debugging and Testing locally
  5. Deploying on cloud (Business Technology Platform)

To make this concrete, we’ll turn the ideas from Part 1 into something you can actually run end to end: A commerce MCP server that wraps Commerce OData APIs as agent‑ready tools.
By the end of Part 2, you will not just understand what a remote MCP server is, you will have one running on your local machine and can be deployed on cloud.

Commerce MCP Server (Architecture and Building Blocks)

The commerce-mcp-server project is a Python‑based MCP server built on FastMCP that exposes a set of tools wrapping Commerce (or any CAP‑based commerce) OData APIs. Instead of calling your Commerce APIs directly from the your agentic solution, the MCP server sits in the middle and offers agent‑ready tools like get_products, get_retailers etc. that are easy for MCP‑aware clients to discover and use. This is the concrete “MCP server” instance that brings the abstract concepts from Part 1 to life.

Building blocks of Commerce MCP Server

  1. Agentic Solutions + MCP Client: This is basically your Enterprise Agentic AI solution which embeds MCP client that talks to the commerce MCP server on behalf of the LLM.  MCP client discovers available tools exposed by the commerce MCP server and routes the LLM's request for data to the MCP server.
  2. Commerce MCP Server: MCP server is the application or a program which exposes the e-commerce capabilities to the AI Agent application through MCP standards.
  3. Tools: Tools are actions that the MCP server can perform. For example: "Get Product List", "Create Cart", "Add Product to Cart" etc. Tools gives host applications a structured set of capabilities.
  4. Transport Layer: MCP uses standard communication like STDIO for local and HTTP with Server‑Sent Events for remote servers.

The diagram below gives a overview of how commerce-mcp-server acts as a facade for the agentic AI solutions.

Jumping into the Code: How to Run It Yourself

Now that we’ve seen the architecture and building blocks, let’s get this MCP server running on your own machine and subaccount. The GitHub project already contains a detailed, step‑by‑step guide. in this section I will highlight the key choices and pitfalls, then point you to the exact README sections to follow.

Step 1: Pre-requisites

Before touching any code, verify the basics as called out in the sections

  1. https://github.com/avinashvaidya09/commerce-mcp-server/blob/main/README.md#pre-requisites
  2. https://github.com/avinashvaidya09/commerce-mcp-server/blob/main/README.md#local-set-up 

If you just want to play with the MCP server without touching BTP, the README’s “Option 1: Mock Backend (no BTP required)” path lets you skip the subaccount and destination setup for now.

Step 2: Decide - mock backend or live backend

The next decision point is: Do you want to hit real Commerce data, or just see the MCP server in action?

  1. For a quick start: Use mock backend as mentioned in the README file.

  2. For an end‑to‑end scenario against BTP:

    1. Use live backend.

    2. Then follow the VCAP SERVICES section to load service credentials into VCAP_SERVICES locally.

If this is your first time, start with Option 1 to understand MCP’s flow. Then come back and switch to Option 2 once your Commerce OData APIs are ready.

Step 3: Start MCP Server

Just start the MCP server to get started - https://github.com/avinashvaidya09/commerce-mcp-server/blob/main/README.md#mcp-server-on-local 

You can see the MCP inspector where you can see the tools and also test them.

 

The good news is: You don’t have to memorize any of these steps. The README file in the repository is intentionally written as a step‑by‑step playbook: from prerequisites and local setup, through mock vs live backend, all the way to BTP deployment and curl‑based debugging. Once you understand the high‑level choices we just walked through, you can simply follow the README top‑to‑bottom and you will end up with a working commerce MCP server and MCP Inspector showing all tools on your local.

Wrapping Up Part 2

At this point, you have seen how the commerce MCP server maps the MCP concepts from Part 1 into a concrete implementation. The architecture diagram and building blocks show you where the MCP server sits between MCP clients and your Commerce OData APIs. Rather than duplicating every command here, the README gives you a detailed, step‑by‑step path to go from zero to a running MCP server locally with mock data or against live Commerce services and finally deployed on BTP.

My recommendation: Read this blog once to get the mental model, then switch to the README and follow it top‑to‑bottom. If you do that, you will finish Part 2 not just with a theoretical understanding, but with a working commerce MCP server, tools like get_products, get_categories etc. exposed via MCP and at least one client (MCP Inspector, helper script, or curl) successfully talking to it.

In Part-3 of the series, we will move from standalone MCP server to integrating it with MCP client. We will create an agent and integrate MCP server, all using Joule Studio.

Disclaimer: This is not an official reference application or documentation. The thoughts outlined in this blog are based on my real world experience and learnings.

Feel free to “like“, “Share“, “Add a Comment” and to get more updates about my next blogs follow me!

References

  1. https://www.anthropic.com/news/model-context-protocol
  2. https://modelcontextprotocol.io/docs/learn/architecture 
  3. https://gofastmcp.com/getting-started/quickstart

entry.title: SAP CAPM Project Lifecycle: From Development to Fiori Preview
entry.content:
Let's see an end  to end demonstration for creating the CAPM project, entity, service with annotations, create the entry via Post man tool and Preview the Application in Fiori.

Below mentioned detailed points covered in this Blog,

  1. BTP Cockpit and its properties
  2. Creation of CAP Project
  3. Linkage of Entity to the HANA DB in the SAP HANA Cloud central
  4. Post man tool Authorization and Post Operation
  5. Preview in the Fiori Application

BTP Cockpit and its properties:

Create a BTP Cock PIT Trial account and Sub Account.

In the Instances and Subscription Add SAP HANA Cloud and SAP Business Application Studio.

Create a Space in the Cloud Foundry, which is a shared, isolated location with an organization (org) used to develop, deploy and manage application and services.

In SAP BTP Cockpit administrators navigate to security->Role collections, create or edit a collection, and add roles-derived from application templates-to define the granular access.

Creation of CAP Project:

In this Business Application studio create a Dev space and create a Project as mentioned below.

 

 

 

Once Project created a project with workspace created. Here I have created ENTITY_CRUD project. As per below image under DB Node we can create the entity and service for the entity create under SRV node.

gen

Auto-generated folder created during build; contains compiled artifacts from your CDS models. You normally don’t edit this manually.

 .vscode

Holds VS Code-specific settings like launch configs and workspace preferences for development.

app

Used for UI/front-end apps (like SAP Fiori); contains HTML, UI5, or other frontend resources.

db

Defines your data model and database artifacts using CDS files (like itemrecordsdb.cds) and deployment configs (undeploy.json).

gen

Another generated folder used during build/deploy; stores intermediate files created by CAP tools.

mta_archives

Contains packaged .mtar files used for deployment to SAP BTP (multi-target application archives).

node_modules

Stores all installed npm dependencies required for your CAP project.

srv

Defines the service layer (business logic & APIs); CDS files like itemrecordssrv.cds expose entities as OData services.

.cdsrc-private.json

Local configuration file for CAP (like profiles, credentials); usually not committed to Git.

.gitignore

Specifies files/folders (like node_modules) that Git should ignore.

 eslint.config.mjs

Configuration for code linting to maintain code quality and standards.

mta.yaml

Defines how the application is structured and deployed in SAP BTP (modules, resources, dependencies).

package.json

Main project config file listing dependencies, scripts, and metadata.

package-lock.json

Locks exact versions of installed dependencies for consistent builds.

 README.md

Documentation file describing the project, setup, and usage.

An entity with Itemrecords created. Here I have used ‘manage, cuid’   from @sap/cds/manage which will create a UUID key for every record creation.

Create a service for itemrecords db which acts as an interface between your data models and outside the world like UI apps or external systems.

Once entity and service generated in the project deploy the project using cf deploy mta_archives/<file>.mtar bash commands. After successful deployment we can see service instances created in the BTP cockpit as shown below.

To link the created entity in BAS to SAP HANA Cloud create a service key for the <entity>-db instance as shown from the below steps.

Choose Entity_CRUD-db and click on the navigation panel where service key need to create. In this illustration I have created as ENTITY_CRUD-db-key.

Once service key generated, we can see the JSON file in the view parameter. This JSON file needs to upload in the SAP HANA explorer to link the DB.

Linkage of Entity to the HANA DB in the SAP HANA Cloud central:

Navigate to Subscribed SAP HANA CLOUD and create instances which allows you to use in-memory computing for real-time transactional and analytical processing. Once instance is created it should be in Running status which indicates Active, available for SQL connections and able to perform data processing, backups and administrative tasks.

Now, open the SAP HANA database Explorer in the SAP HANA cloud central, click on the + icon where instance type to be chosen as HDI container and choose Apply from JSON string where need to paste the JSON file from the service key entity in the BTP cockpit.

 

 

 

 

Once JSON file applied Entity from the BAS linked to the SAP HANA Cloud central.

 

 

 

Post man tool Authorization and Post Operation:

To Authenticate authorization in the Post Man tool go to <entity>-auth in the BTP cockpit as shown below.

 

 

Here I have navigated to the ENTITY_CRUD-auth where service key SK-ENTITY_CRUD is created once service key created a JSON file generated with client ID, client secret, URL.

 

 

 

 

 

 

Client ID, URL client secret mapped to the Authorization in the post man tool and generate the Access Token.

 

 

 

 

Using post operation can create a data to the entity in the CAP project.

 

 

 

Successful created entries showing in the SAP HANA Cloud central.

 

 Preview in the Fiori Application:

To Preview the data in the Fiori application, In the CAP project search with Open Application Generator and create a List Report page.

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

Once Application created, In the project terminal using Cds watch –profile hybrid can view the preview of the Application in Fiori

Required Annotations can use in the srv which will be reflect in the Fiori Application.

 

Conclusion
This demonstration showcased the seamless integration between SAP Business Application Studio, SAP HANA Cloud, and the Fiori front-end. By leveraging annotations and the CAP service layer, we successfully created a functional CRUD application with automated UUID management and secure API access. You are now ready to extend this project with custom business logic or complex UI enhancements.

entry.title: SAP Developer News for April 23rd, 2026
entry.content:
ITEMS 

Migration tool for your SAP Build Apps frontend web applications 

  • Blog post https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blog-posts-by-sap/migration-tool-for-your-sap-build-apps-frontend-web-applications/ba-p/14368805  
  • SAP Help page https://help.sap.com/docs/build-apps/service-guide/sap-build-apps-path-forward#migrating-from-the-sap-build-apps-standalone-offering-to-sap-build 

SAP Open Source Webinar: Olympic Sailing on Eclipse Technology – From Closed Source to Open Source 

  • Event info https://events.sap.com/olympic-sailing-on-eclipse-technology-from-closed-source-to-open/en_us/home.html  

SAP BTP Platform Automation Roundtable #3 

  • Blog post https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blog-posts-by-sap/customer-amp-partner-roundtable-for-sap-btp-platform-automation-3-april/ba-p/14374109  

DYK #13 Override default auth while using xsuaa (CAP) 

  • Capire: Authentication in Production – Enforced by Default https://cap.cloud.sap/docs/node.js/authentication#enforced-by-default  

CHAPTER TITLES 

00:00 Intro 
00:07 Migration tool for your SAP Build Apps frontend web applications 
01:31 SAP Open Source Webinar: Olympic Sailing on Eclipse Technology – From Closed Source to Open Source 
02:24 SAP BTP Platform Automation Roundtable #3 
03:24 DYK #13 Override default auth while using xsuaa (CAP) 

TRANSCRIPT

[Intro]
This is the SAP Developer News for April the 23rd, 2026.

[Shrini]
Hello, SAP community. By now, you may have seen the announcement about SAP Build Apps deprecation as of March 2026. Understandably, the first question on everyone's mind is, what happens to the apps I am already building? The team has been listening closely to the feedback from the community. To make this transition as seamless as possible, SAP has rolled out a migration tool tool for your SAP Build Apps projects. This tool will transform your existing front-end web applications into a fully structured React or SAP UI5 project. Once transformed, you can easily continue enhancing them within your SAP Build environment using Business Application Studio. If you are ready to migrate, you can reach out to SAP by opening a support ticket with the mtar file of your project, specify whether the app should be based on React or SAPUI5 and authorize the transformation. Detailed instructions on creating this ticket are available on this help page. The link can be found in the description. This tool was built based on your feedback. Let us know how this works for you. Finally, a quick reminder, you will continue to have full access to SAP BuildApps for the entire duration of your current contract.

[Mamikee]
Sailing Analytics is now open source, and if you want to learn more about the journey behind it, there's an upcoming webinar you don't want to miss. On May 20th, 2026 at 3pm CEST, join the technical webinar on Eclipse Azimuth Sailing Analytics, a real-time regatta tracking and analysis tool that has been used at more than 1,000 sailing events, including the Olympic Games. In the session, product owner Axel will walk through SAP's journey to open source the solution, including licensing strategy, internal processing, collaboration with the Eclipse Foundation, and community building efforts. Now, if you're interested in open source, real-world analytics solutions, or how projects like this evolve beyond SAP, this is definitely one to check out. You can learn more and register in the link below.

[DJ]
Are you interested in the Terraform and CLI tools for managing your BTP resources? Sharing opinions and experiences on this topic with others? Then the BTP Platform Automation Customer and Partner Roundtable series is for you. And the third instance of the series is just around the corner. It's on Tuesday, the 12th of May at As usual, there will be some info on latest updates, a round of Q&A, some demos and more. There's a blog
post with further details, a link that you can use to register. It's free, by the way. And also in that blog post, there's a link to content from the previous two instances. See you there.

[Shrini]
Did you know you can override the default authentication behavior for CAP service endpoints using a simple feature flag? By default, when you deploy a CAP service to Cloud Foundry with a production -grade authentication strategy, all service endpoints are protected. This happens because CAP implicitly adds the @requires authenticated user annotation to every service. But what if you want to make some endpoints publicly accessible, especially during testing or for specific use cases? You can disable this default behavior by adding the flag RestrictAllServicesToFalse to your package.json. In this batch request service, setting the RestrictAllServices flag to false makes all service endpoints publicly accessible by default, meaning no authentication is required. However, the createBatch action is explicitly protected using the @RequiresAuthenticatedUser annotation, ensuring that only authenticated users can invoke it.

entry.title: Build Solutions for SAP Cloud ERP with confidence
entry.content:
Partner Reference Application Family provides Partners with a “golden path” to becoming a SaaS provider of multi-tenant applications based on the SAP Business Technology Platform (SAP BTP). The guidance covers building, running, and integrating scalable full-stack cloud applications. Whether you are building a brand-new SaaS product or upgrading an existing one, these resources act as a blueprint for success.

It provides ready-to-run code branches and tutorials to accelerate the development of scalable SaaS solutions. It consists of multiple public SAP-samples repositories on GitHub, offering comprehensive guidance aligned with the “principle of one” and “clean core”. These resources highlight the value of the extensibility environment, demonstrate best practices in code quality and architecture, and serve as demo-ready examples for realistic partner extensions.

CAP Partner Reference Application
A GitHub repository featuring the sample multi-tenant solution “Poetry Slam Manager” built on SAP BTP CAP, along with detailed tutorials for developers and application managers.

When delivering standardized SaaS solutions to multiple customers, customer-specific requirements are inevitable. Using SAP Build and CAP, the solution is extensible out of the box, enabling easy customization. The companion repository, "Partner Reference Application Extension", provides guidance on building and deploying such extensions, including a sample customer-specific extension (“Catering Management”).

ABAP Partner Reference Application

For building ABAP-based side-by-side multi-tenant applications for SAP Cloud ERP, refer to the end-to-end guide. It includes the sample solution “Music Festival Manager” based on ABAP Cloud.

Cross Stack Partner Reference Application
Designed for scenarios requiring on-stack extensions on SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition with seamless integration to SAP BTP CAP applications.
This example demonstrates how an on-stack extension integrates with a side-by-side CAP multi-tenant application (Poetry Slam Manager). It includes tutorials to help build and integrate both components, enabling enterprise-grade cross-stack extensions using ABAP Cloud and CAP.

On-Stack Partner Reference Application
Focused on delivering on-stack extensions for SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition with scalability, upgrade stability, and seamless integration.
This application integrates with Manage Sales Orders – Version 2 (SAP Fiori app) and showcases event-driven loyalty program management. It demonstrates how standard SAP business processes can be enhanced without modifying the core. Top of Form

 

The Partner Reference Application Family is your accelerator for building next-generation SAP solutions. It’s not just about code—it’s about enabling partners to deliver scalable, extensible, and future-proof applications with speed and quality. By leveraging these ready-made blueprints and best practices, you can reduce development effort, ensure clean core compliance, and focus on what truly matters: delivering value to your customers.

entry.title: Enhancing Enterprise Applications: AI-powered User Experience with SAP CAP AI Plugin
entry.content:
In today’s world, users expect their applications to smartly reduce their manual effort and help them complete their tasks quickly.

And enterprise applications are no exception. Imagine looking at a huge, blank form that you must fill out to complete a mundane but mandatory task at the office. You can practically feel the energy drain out of your body as the form stares back at you. Suddenly, a coffee break looks enticing 🙂

But what if your application could generate recommendations based on the data available in the system, and dramatically cut your effort to just eyeballing the correctness of most of the fields, only requiring you to fill in a few?

While this may be on every user’s wish list, implementing these AI-driven smart features in existing (or even new) applications can require a lot of development effort.

At SAP Cloud Application Programming (CAP), we believe in reducing boilerplate code and letting developers focus on intent. Hence, in the April release of CAP, we have released the SAP CAP AI plugin for node.js. (Watch this space for the Java version, which will be released soon).

This plugin will enable existing and new CAP applications to use recommendations generated by the RPT-1 Generative AI model provided by SAP AI Core. The recommendations will then be pre-filled in the UI of the application. All this is out-of-the-box, without writing a single line of code.

The CAP AI plugin does three main things:

  1. Simplifies AI Core configuration
  2. Provide recommendations on the UI coming from RPT-1
  3. Inherits the best of CAP

Simplifying AI Core configuration –

Admin tasks such as creating a new SAP AI Core resource group per tenant and creating an RPT-1 deployment are automated using the “AICore” service created by the plugin.

Recommendations in the UI application –

Any entity with a value help (@Common.ValueList on the property or @CDS.odata.valuelist on the association target) will be used by the plugin to show the recommendations.

 

entity Books {
  key ID : Integer;
  title  : String(111);
  descr  : String(1111);
  genre : Association to one Genres;
  status : Association to one Status;
}
annotate Genres with @cds.odata.valuelist;
annotate Books with {
    status @Common.ValueList : {
        CollectionPath : 'Status',
        Parameters: [
            {
                $Type: 'Common.ValueListParameterInOut'
                ValueListProperty : 'code',
                LocalDataProperty : status_code
            }
        ]
    }
}

 

 

The best of CAP

All the battle-tested enterprise readiness of CAP is inherently part of the plugin. It also supports multi-tenancy, and local testing without an SAP AI Core instance.

You can read more about the plugin here - https://github.com/cap-js/ai

We are excited to have you try out this plugin.  If you have any feature requests or suggestions, please feel free to reach out to us.

Till then, happy coding!

entry.title: Introducing ATUL Architecture : A New Blog Series on SAP BTP for Intelligent Utilities
entry.content:
Over the past few years, I’ve had the chance to work closely with utilities that are modernizing their operations using SAP S/4HANA, SAP BTP, and event‑driven, real‑time architectures. One thing has become very clear: Utilities need simple, practical architecture patterns — not product brochures, not theory, and not marketing slides.

So I’m starting a new blog series called ATUL Architecture, where I’ll share practical patterns, diagrams, and lessons learned from real projects. The goal is simple: Make SAP BTP architecture easier to understand and easier to apply in the utilities industry.

This series is for architects, consultants, developers, and anyone working on SAP‑based utility transformation.

What the Series Will Cover

Here’s the plan for the first six posts:

ATUL Architecture #1 — Why Utilities Need a Digital Core on SAP BTP

  • Clean core

  • Integration strategy

  • Why BTP becomes the “digital backbone”

  • A simple digital‑core diagram

This post sets the foundation for the entire series.

 

ATUL Architecture #2 — Event‑Driven Utilities: Designing Real‑Time Flows on SAP BTP

  • Event Mesh

  • AMI → BTP → S/4HANA flow

  • Real‑time vs batch

  • A simple event‑flow diagram

This will focus on how utilities can move from slow batch processes to real‑time operations.

 

ATUL Architecture #3 — Bridging OT and IT: Bringing Grid Data into SAP BTP Safely

  • IEC 61850

  • MQTT

  • Substation → BTP patterns

  • Security considerations

This post will show how OT data can be brought into SAP BTP in a safe and scalable way.

 

ATUL Architecture #4 — AI on SAP BTP for Grid Operations

  • Time‑series data

  • Anomaly detection

  • Predictive maintenance

  • AI → workflow → S/4HANA integration

This will focus on how AI becomes actionable inside SAP processes.

 

ATUL Architecture #5 — Designing a Utility Data Fabric on SAP BTP

  • Data lake

  • Semantic models

  • Governance

  • Analytics for grid + customer

This post will cover how utilities can build a unified data layer on BTP.

 

ATUL Architecture #6 — Intelligent Utility Blueprint: End‑to‑End Architecture on SAP BTP

  • One consolidated diagram

  • End‑to‑end flow

  • Real‑time + AI + automation

  • Business outcomes

This will be the “big picture” post — the one that ties everything together.

I Want Your Input

Before I publish the first post, I’d love to hear from the SAP Community:

  • What topics would you like to see in this series?

  • Are there specific SAP BTP patterns you want explained?

  • Any challenges you’re facing in utilities, integration, or real‑time operations?

  • Should I include more diagrams, code snippets, or architecture trade‑offs?

Your suggestions will help shape the series.

What’s Next

I’ll publish ATUL Architecture #1 — Why Utilities Need a Digital Core on SAP BTP next. It will include a simple diagram and a practical explanation of why BTP becomes the digital backbone for modern utilities.

Looking forward to your ideas and feedback.

entry.title: Product-Agent Fit: A Practical Framework for Building on SAP BTP in the Age of Joule
entry.content:
A practical framework for SAP architects, developers, and product managers navigating the agentic enterprise

The Question SAP Is Already Asking

At SAP TechEd 2025 in Berlin, SAP Executive Board Member Muhammad Alam, in charge of SAP Product & Engineering, said SAP was entering “the era of agentic AI, where AI moves from being just a tool to becoming your trusted teammate."

A few months later, on March 31, 2026, SAP CEO Christian Klein went further in his blog: "Software is becoming the operating system for trusted autonomy." (Klein, March: “Software is Entering Its Most Powerful Era”)

These are not marketing statements. They signal architectural direction — and they have direct implications for every SAP developer, consultant, and product manager building on BTP today.

Here is the question they force us to ask about every application we build or extend:

Not only "will a user love this?" — but also "will Joule reach for it?"

That stretch — from human satisfaction to agent invocability — is what I want to explore in this post through a framework I have been developing: Product-Agent Fit (PAF).

 

What Is Product-Agent Fit?

For nearly two decades, the dominant lens for evaluating software products has been Product-Market Fit (PMF) — the degree to which a product satisfies a human market's needs well enough that users adopt it, pay for it, and recommend it. PMF assumes the validator of product value is always a human being with preferences, emotions, and tolerance for rough edges.

Product-Agent Fit (PAF) proposes an additional validator: the AI agent.

PAF is the degree to which a product's capability surface is naturally consumable by AI agents — such that agents discover it autonomously, invoke it correctly, trust its outputs, chain it into workflows, and operate within enterprise governance constraints — without human mediation at invocation time.

In SAP terms: does your CAP application, your Fiori app, your S/4HANA extension — does it work when Joule calls it? Not when a user clicks through it. When an agent invokes it.

The answer to that question is increasingly one of the most important singlas you would know about the long-term relevance of whatever you are building.

 

Why This Matters Specifically on SAP BTP

SAP has been executing a clear, multi-year trajectory toward agent-first architecture:

  • September 2023: Joule launched as a copilot — human-facing, UI-adjacent
  • October 2024: Joule went agentic — multi-agent orchestration, coordinating "across business functions" (TechEd 2024)
  • April 2025: SAP joined Google as a founding industry contributor to the A2A (Agent-to-Agent) protocol — standardizing how agents communicate across enterprise platforms
  • November 2025: SAP unveiled major advancements in agentic AI, including introducing new Model Context Protocol (MCP) servers at TechEd 2025, enabling Claude Code, Cursor, Cline, and Windsurf to access SAP development frameworks directly
  • March 2026: Christian Klein: "Building an agent is becoming increasingly easier—the tip of the iceberg. Deploying it across end-to-end supply chains or financial close processes, with full compliance and audit trails, is where most of the effort lies."

The direction is unambiguous. SAP is rebuilding its platform so that Joule — and agents beyond Joule — can consume every capability without depending on screen navigation.

The question for us as builders is: are the things we build on BTP keeping pace with that direction?

 

The PAF Framework — Mapped to SAP

I propose eleven dimensions for evaluating whether an SAP BTP application has achieved Product-Agent Fit. Each maps to specific SAP tools and patterns you can act on today.

Two SAP resources serve as the primary references throughout this framework. For agent-layer implementation — Joule Studio, MCP tooling, agent architecture, and the AI development lifecycle — SAP's AI Golden Path (architecture.learning.sap.com/docs/aigp) is the dedicated guide. For platform security, CAP patterns, ORD, and audit logging — the SAP BTP Developer's Guide applies. Where a dimension touches both layers, both are called out.

1. Capability Fit

Are your capabilities atomic enough for an agent to invoke directly?

In SAP terms: your OData V4 services, your CAP action handlers, your ABAP logic exposed as OData V4 services via RAP (ABAP RESTful Application Programming Model) — are they scoped at the level of a single, invocable agent action? Not "manage the purchase order" but release_purchase_order(po_id, approver_id).

The 2,100+ pre-delivered Joule skills SAP has published by the end of 2025 are each scoped exactly this way. If you are building custom extensions, your capability granularity should match that standard.

SAP tool: CAP action definitions, OData V4 services built on RAP

2. Interface Fit

Is your API surface natively machine-readable without human explanation?

This is where MCP comes in. At TechEd 2025, SAP announced that developers using agentic coding tools can now access SAP development frameworks via SAP Build local MCP Servers. The open-source SAP MDK MCP Server (github.com/SAP/mdk-mcp-server) exposes Mobile Development Kit tools as MCP tools consumable by authorized MCP-compatible agents.

For your custom BTP application, Interface Fit means: is there a machine-readable, typed schema that describes what your service does, what parameters it takes, and what errors it returns — in a form an LLM can reason over without a human explaining it?

SAP tools: MCP Server definitions, OData V4 with typed EDM schemas, SAP AI SDK (Javascript: github.com/SAP/ai-sdk-js; Java: github.com/SAP/ai-sdk-java)

3. Reliability Fit

Are your outputs deterministic enough for agents to chain without verification overhead?

Christian Klein stated this precisely in his March 2026 blog post: "orchestration, policy enforcement, and workflow determinism are the gatekeepers of trust."

In practice, this means your business logic — especially anything involving financial calculations, inventory, or compliance — must be deterministic and auditable. Joule should call your service and trust the result. It should not need to re-verify the output or ask a human to confirm before passing it to the next step.

This is the separation-of-concerns principle that CAP embodies, now applied to agent consumption: your service layer handles determinism; Joule handles the reasoning. Do not mix these responsibilities.

SAP tool: CAP handlers with deterministic business logic, CDS-defined validation rules

4. Context Fit

Can Joule determine when and how to invoke your service from its schema alone?

SAP's Knowledge Graph (announced TechEd 2024) maps "relationships and context across SAP's vast data landscape" using fifty years of business process expertise — so Joule knows the semantics of your business domain, not just the syntax of your API.

For your custom application, this means: tool descriptions must be semantically rich enough for an LLM to correctly select your tool from a catalog of hundreds. A tool named processMaterial with no description will not be selected. A tool named check_material_availability with the description "Returns current stock level and ATP date for a given material in a specified plant" will be.

SAP tools: MCP tool descriptions, Joule Studio skill definitions, CDS annotations

5. Composability Fit

Does your service output feed directly into the next Joule step without transformation?

SAP's industry partnership in the A2A protocol (April 2025) is a direct investment in this dimension. A2A is intended to enable Joule to coordinate with agents from Google, Microsoft, and other vendors — "sharing context and acting together across enterprise platforms without requiring manual system switching or data loss."

For your application: every service response should be structured JSON that the next tool in the chain can consume directly. If your response is only a formatted HTML string or a PDF attachment, Joule cannot chain it — a human must intervene. That is a composability failure.

SAP tools: Structured CDS projections, A2A-compliant agent registration, Joule Studio orchestration

6. Headless Deployment Fit

Does your application deliver full value without a human opening a browser?

SAP repositioned Joule from AI copilot to "AI orchestrator across your entire organization" (February 2025) — meaning Joule should be able to execute complete workflows end-to-end, not just assist a human who is already on a screen.

Ask this about your application: if I remove the Fiori UI entirely, can Joule still accomplish the core business task? If the answer is no — because some step requires a human to click Confirm in the UI — that is a Headless Deployment Fit gap.

The fix is not to remove the UI. It is to ensure that human confirmation steps are exposed as structured approval actions that agents can surface through conversation, not as UI buttons that block the workflow.

SAP tools: CAP action handlers callable by MCP, Joule-invocable approval flows on SAP Build Work Zone

7. Trust Pipeline Fit

Does your application enforce governance at the API layer, not just the UI layer?

Walter Sun, SVP and Global Head of AI at SAP, has been direct: "accountability must always rest with humans or legal entities, never with the AI itself" (Sun, June 2025: “From Assistive AI to Agentic AI: Risks, Responsibilities, and the Road Ahead”). And Christian Klein identifies "validation rules, compliance checks, approval flows, identity management and audit trails" as non-negotiable requirements for enterprise agents (Klein, March 2026).

In practice, this means authorization enforcement must happen at your CAP service layer — not just at the Fiori UI. For new applications, this applies whether you are using XSUAA or the newer SAP Cloud Identity Services (IAS) and Authorization Management Service (AMS) stack that the SAP BTP Developer's Guide now recommends; the governance requirement is identical under both. An agent that bypasses the UI must encounter the same authorization gates as a human user. Every agent-invoked write should be logged to the SAP Audit Log Service with full provenance.

If your authorization logic lives only in the Fiori app and your CAP services are essentially open to any authenticated caller, you have a Trust Pipeline Fit gap — and a serious security risk in an agentic world.

SAP tools: Authorization enforcement in CAP handlers, SAP Audit Log Service, SAP Authorization and Trust Management

8. Longitudinal Context Fit

Does your application give Joule access to accumulated business knowledge, not just current-state data?

SAP's Business Data Cloud creates "a single trusted data layer" across SAP and non-SAP sources. The SAP Knowledge Graph leverages "50-plus years of business process expertise" to give agents semantic understanding of business relationships that raw data cannot provide.

For your custom application: does Joule know that this supplier has had three late deliveries in the past six months? Does it know that this customer's contract expires in 30 days? Does it understand the regulatory history of this tax position? If that context is locked in a data warehouse that Joule cannot query, your application cannot achieve longitudinal context fit.

SAP tools: SAP Business Data Cloud, SAP HANA Cloud Vector Engine (for RAG), SAP HANA Cloud Knowledge Graph Engine, SAP Analytics Cloud, CDS-defined analytical views

9. Surface-Agnostic Rendering Fit

Does your application reach the human wherever they are, not only inside Fiori?

Joule has been extended beyond SAP applications into third-party applications via WalkMe integration (SAP Sapphire, 2025), and Slack adoption for SAP agent interactions has grown significantly. The underlying principle is the same one Salesforce articulates for headless commerce and CRM — that the engagement surface can change (mobile app, Slack, Teams, voice interface) while the underlying business platform stays consistent: "The surface changes. The platform doesn't."

For your application: can the agent surface your workflow outcomes as a Slack notification, a Teams card, a mobile push alert — without you having to build a separate integration for each? If your application only surfaces results in Fiori, you are missing the engagement surfaces where your users actually spend their time.

SAP tools: SAP Build Work Zone, Joule in Slack integration, SAP Mobile Services, SAP BTP notification services

10. Agent Operational Maturity Fit

Can you detect and fix Joule behavioral drift without waiting for a user complaint?

SAP ships the LeanIX AI Agent Hub — dashboards showing agent deployment, process impact, and performance metrics. This is the SAP-side equivalent of what Salesforce calls its Testing Center and Observability tooling.

The critical insight from the industry: agents are probabilistic, not deterministic. There is no bug to fix — only behavior to observe and tune. If your Joule skill works correctly today but drifts after the next model update or business rule change, you need operational tooling to detect that drift before it affects production processes.

SAP tools: LeanIX AI Agent Hub, SAP AI Core evaluation service, Joule Studio agent testing

11. Marketplace Discovery Fit

Is your application discoverable by agents, not just by humans browsing the SAP Marketplace?

ORD — Open Resource Discovery is SAP's answer to this dimension. Now governed by the Linux Foundation Europe and NeoNephos Foundation (open-resource-discovery.org), ORD enables applications to "self-describe their exposed resources and capabilities" in a standardized, decentralized format. When your BTP application publishes ORD metadata, Joule can discover and compose your capabilities without a developer manually wiring the integration.

If your application is not publishing ORD metadata, it is invisible to the next generation of agent-based orchestration — regardless of how well it works when a human finds it manually.

SAP tools: ORD specification implementation in CAP, SAP Business Accelerator Hub publication, Joule Studio as a unified catalog and registration system 

 

The SAP PAF Maturity Ladder

Where does your application sit today?

Level 0 — INVISIBLE

  UI-only. No OData/CAP API. Joule cannot reach it.

  Example: Legacy Excel-based processes, classic Dynpro without API layer.

 

Level 1 — REACHABLE

  OData API exists but no ORD metadata, no MCP tools defined.

  Joule can be manually wired but won't discover it autonomously.

  Example: Standard CAP app without ORD publication.

 

Level 2 — CALLABLE

  OData V4 with typed schemas.

 Joule can invoke with explicit developer instruction. Errors are structured.

  Example: CAP app following the SAP BTP Developer's Guide, ORD partially configured.

 

Level 3 — DISCOVERABLE

  ORD metadata published. MCP tools defined in line with the SAP AI Golden Path.

  Joule Studio or other agent tooling can discover and wire the capability with much less manual effort.

  Example: Full SAP BTP Developer's Guide + SAP AI Golden Path + MCP + ORD.

 

Level 4 — PREFERRED

  Joule selects autonomously. Authorization enforced at CAP layer.

  Human-in-the-Loop (HITL) approval flows agent-accessible.

  Longitudinal context available via Business Data Cloud.

  Registered as A2A-compatible via SAP Agent Fabric / BTP agent runtime.

  Example: Production Joule Agent with full BTP governance.

 

Level 5 — FOUNDATIONAL

  Other Joule agents and third-party agents depend on yours as a building block.

  Discoverable within Joule Studio without manual wiring.

  Example: What SAP's own HANA Cloud and Master Data services are candidates to become.

 

What to Do This Sprint

With your application's level on the PAF Maturity Ladder identified, here is a concrete sprint-level action plan for each transition.

Moving from Level 1 to Level 2: Publish your CAP service with a typed OData V4 schema. Define structured error responses using CAP's error handling framework. Ensure every action has a meaningful, LLM-readable description in your CDS annotations.

Moving from Level 2 to Level 3: Implement ORD metadata in your CAP application. Define at least one MCP tool wrapping your most-used CAP action. SAP's AI Golden Path (architecture.learning.sap.com/docs/aigp) is the recommended starting point for MCP tool definitions, Joule Studio registration, and the agent development lifecycle. Test that Joule can invoke your tool without a human explaining the parameters, with structured errors and audit trace (in natural language).

Moving from Level 3 to Level 4: Move authorization enforcement into your CAP service handlers — not just Fiori app routing. Add Audit Log Service integration for every agent-invoked write. Design your human approval flows as CAP actions that return structured confirmation tokens, not (just) as UI buttons. Register your agent as A2A-compatible via SAP Agent Fabric or the BTP agent runtime to enable cross-vendor agent interoperability.

Moving from Level 4 to Level 5: Document your MCP tool definitions in a format LLMs can parse. Publish relevant APIs, packages, or reusable assets where appropriate. Consider submitting your Joule skill to the SAP partner ecosystem so other agent stacks can discover and reuse your capability.

 

The Framing That Changes How You Build

I want to close with Christian Klein's formulation, because I think it is the most important framing for every SAP practitioner today:

"Building an agent is becoming increasingly easier—the tip of the iceberg. Deploying it across end-to-end supply chains or financial close processes, with full compliance and audit trails, is where most of the effort lies."

That sentence captures the challenge precisely. The hard part is not building the agent. The hard part is making your platform ready for the agent — so that when Joule arrives at your CAP service, your OData endpoint, your approval workflow, it finds something it can invoke, trust, govern, and chain.

That readiness is Product-Agent Fit.

And based on the trajectory of SAP's own platform — A2A, MCP, ORD, Joule Studio, LeanIX AI Agent Hub — it is clear that SAP is building many PAF-like requirements into the platform layer. The question is whether the applications we build on top of that platform are keeping pace.

Over to You

I am curious where the SAP community currently stands on this:

  • Are you already designing your BTP applications with Joule invocability in mind, or is agent consumption still a future concern?
  • Has your team published ORD metadata or defined MCP tools yet?
  • Where do most of your current applications sit on the PAF Maturity Ladder?

Drop your thoughts in the comments. The more specific the better — I would love to hear concrete patterns, blockers, and solutions from those already building at the frontier.

entry.title: CAP Developers - Call to Action to mitigate and Apply Solution provided in SAP Note 3747787
entry.content:
On April 29, 2026, four malicious open-source package versions were distributed into the NPM ecosystem. These malicious versions appear to exfiltrate information, such as credentials, and attempt to propagate into downstream software packages as well as adjacent software repositories when installed on a system.

If you are uncertain whether your systems have been affected, it is crucial to act promptly. Begin by following the mitigation steps outlined to maintain your environment’s security. Promptly taking these actions will help protect your systems and data from potential risks.

Other Terms

  • MBT
  • NPM
  • CAP
  • SAP Cloud Application Programming Model
  • MTA Build Tool

List of compromised NPM package versions:

  • · @cap-js/sqlite - v2.2.2
  • · @cap-js/postgres - v2.2.2
  • · @cap-js/db-service - v2.10.1
  • · mbt@1.2.48

Solution

If you have identified that you may be affected, perform the following measures via provided SAP Note below:

https://me.sap.com/notes/0003747787

SAP Support Ticket Component : BC-XS-CDX-NJS

Potential references:

  • https://www.aikido.dev/blog/mini-shai-hulud-has-appeared
  • https://www.stepsecurity.io/blog/a-mini-shai-hulud-has-appeared
  • https://www.mend.io/blog/shai-hulud-sap-cap-supply-chain-attack-claude-code/
  • https://onapsis.com/blog/sap-cap-mini-shai-hulud-supply-chain-attack/
  • https://snyk.io/de/blog/bun-based-stealer-hits-sap-cap-js-mbt-npm-packages/

entry.title: Dynamic authorisation with user attributes in SAP CAP and AMS
entry.content:
Introduction: The Business Challenge

Your company just acquired a new business unit. Overnight, 500 users need access to sales data, but only for their division. Doing it with role-based access control means creating roles like Sales_Manager_EMEA, Sales_Manager_APAC, Sales_Manager_LATAM, and countless variations. As dimensions multiply (division, region, department, cost center), you face role explosion—hundreds or thousands of roles that don't scale.

There's a better way with attribute-based authorisations. With Authorisation Management Service (AMS) and Cloud Identity Services, you define a single policy with a restriction:

Give READ access where division = $user.division

The $user.division pulls from the authenticated user's profile. Authorisation administrators manage policies through the Cloud Identity Services console—no code changes, no deployments. When a sales manager transfers from EMEA to APAC, HR updates the division attribute in the corporate identity system, and access automatically adjusts across all applications. No role re-assignment required.

Understanding the Technical Foundation

Building on recent SAP Community blogs on AMS (here and here), let's focus on attribute-based authorization.

The stack:

  • Cloud Identity Services authenticates users and stores attributes (division, cost center, region)
  • AMS manages and evaluates authorisation policies at runtime
  • CAP applications enforce authorisation using user attributes for dynamic filtering

Traditional RBAC vs. attribute-based:

  • Traditional: Sales_Manager_EMEA, Sales_Manager_APAC, Sales_Manager_LATAM → doesn't scale
  • Attribute-based: where division = $user.division → dynamic, adapts automatically

The runtime flow:

  1. User logs in through corporate IdP (e.g., Microsoft Entra ID)
  2. IAS returns JWT with custom attributes (division, warehouse, etc.)
  3. CAP extracts attributes and makes them available to AMS
  4. Database queries are automatically filtered based on user attributes
  5. User sees only data they're authorised to access

This approach delivers measurable business benefits: significant reduction in "access change" requests to IT teams, and a single source of truth in IAS for access decisions with complete audit trail for compliance.

Implementation Walkthrough: The Complete Picture

Let's walk through the actual implementation:

  1. IAS Configuration: Configure custom attributes in Cloud Identity Services
  2. AMS Schema: Declare available user attributes in the schema
  3. CAP Server Integration: Extract attributes from JWT and map to CAP & AMS user object
  4. Authorisation Policies: Create policies in Cloud Identity Services with attribute-based restrictions

Step 1: Configure Custom Attributes in Cloud Identity Services

Business context: Your organization structures access by division and warehouse. Users should only access data related to their assigned division and warehouse location.

Configuration steps:

  1. Navigate to IAS Admin Console → Applications → Your Application
  2. Under the Trust → Attributes tab, add new attributes:
    • Name: division
    • Source: Cloud Identity Services or the corporate identity provider

Do the same for the warehouse attribute.

Example user attributes:

  • alice@example.com → division: "LOG", warehouse: "MAD"
  • bob@example.com → division: "RET", warehouse: "BCN"

Verify the attribute in JWT:

After authentication, decode the JWT token (e.g., at jwt.io) and you will find:

 
{
	"sub": "alice@example.com",
	"division": "LOG",
	"warehouse": "MAD"
}

This confirms the custom attribute is flowing from IAS into your application's authentication token.

Step 2: Define User Attributes in AMS Schema

Before using $user.division or $user.warehouse in policies, you must declare it in the AMS schema. This tells the AMS runtime what attributes are available for policy evaluation.

Edit ams/dcl/schema.dcl:

 
SCHEMA {
	"$user": {
		groups: String,
		division: String,
		warehouse: String
	}
}

What this does:

  • Declares the structure of user attributes available in policy expressions
  • division and warehouse are now recognized by the DCL compiler
  • Enables autocomplete and validation in policy files

Step 3: Map JWT Token Attributes to AMS Input

The critical piece: AMS needs to extract custom attributes from the JWT token and make them available to policy evaluation. This is where you connect the IAS attributes to the CAP/AMS authorisation context.

Create a custom auth provider in srv/server.js:

 
const cds = require('@sap/cds');
const { amsCapPluginRuntime, IdentityServiceAuthProvider } = require("@sap/ams");

class CustomAuthProvider extends IdentityServiceAuthProvider {
/**
*  {import("@sap/xssec").IdentityServiceSecurityContext} securityContext
*/
	getInput(securityContext) {
		const defaultInput = super.getInput(securityContext);
		// Extract custom attribute from JWT token payload
		const division = securityContext.token.payload.division;
		if (division) {
			defaultInput["$user.division"] = division;
		}
		// You can add more attributes here
		const warehouse = securityContext.token.payload.warehouse;
		if (warehouse) {
			defaultInput["$user.warehouse"] = warehouse;
		}
		return defaultInput;
	}
}

// Register the custom auth provider
amsCapPluginRuntime.authProvider.xssecAuthProvider = new CustomAuthProvider(amsCapPluginRuntime.ams);

What this does:

  • Extends AMS's IdentityServiceAuthProvider to intercept token processing
  • Extracts division and warehouse from the JWT payload (e.g.: securityContext.token.payload.division)
  • Maps it to $user.division for use in DCL policies and CAP restrictions
  • Attributes are now available throughout your authorisation logic

Verification: You can log the attribute to confirm it's accessible:

 
// In srv/service.js
this.before('READ', 'Orders', async (req) => {
	console.log('User division:', req.user.attr.division || 'not set');
	console.log('User warehouse:', req.user.attr.warehouse || 'not set');
});

Step 4: Create Authorisation Policies with User Attribute Restrictions

Now that attributes are flowing from IAS → JWT → CAP/AMS context, you can create authorisation policies that reference them.

Cloud Identity Services Authorisation Policies Approach

Navigate to Cloud Identity Services → Applications → Your Application → Authorisation Policies to create policies with attribute-based restrictions.

Creating a DivisionReader policy:

  1. In the Cloud Identity Services admin console, open your application
  2. Navigate to the Authorisation Policies tab
  3. Create a new restriction (e.g., ReaderByDivision) based on an existing restriction (Reader in the example below)
  4. Add a Restriction: division = $user.division
  5. Assign users or groups to this policy

What happens at runtime:

  1. User with division: "LOG" requests GET /orders
  2. AMS evaluates the DivisionReader policy with $user.division = "LOG"
  3. The restriction division = $user.division becomes division = "LOG"
  4. CAP injects WHERE division = 'LOG' into the SQL query
  5. User only sees orders for the Logistics & Distribution division
  6. The same query executed by a user with division: "RET" automatically returns only Retail Operations orders

CAP Service restriction annotations approach

For developers who prefer to define authorisation rules in code (especially during development), you can also reference $user.division directly in CAP service definitions:

 
service OrderService {
	@restrict: [{
		grant: ['READ'],
		to: ['ReadOrders'],
		where: 'division = $user.division'
	}]
	entity Orders as projection on data.Orders;
	@restrict: [{
		grant: ['READ'],
		to: ['ReadInventory'],
		where: 'warehouse = $user.warehouse'
	}]
	entity Inventory as projection on data.Inventory;
}

Benefits and Separation of Concerns

This attribute-based authorisation approach delivers concrete advantages through clear separation of responsibilities:

For Developers: Build the technical foundation once

  • Configure attribute extraction from JWT tokens
  • Declare available attributes in the AMS schema
  • Deploy the application

For Authorisation Administrators: Manage access through the UI

  • Create policies in Cloud Identity Services
  • Define attribute-based restrictions without coding
  • Modify policies as organizational needs change—no deployments required

For HR/Identity Teams: Maintain user attributes as single source of truth

  • Update user profiles when employees change roles or divisions
  • Attributes automatically sync to Cloud Identity Services with Identity Provisioning
  • Access adjusts across all integrated applications

References

Check out the ams-samples-node repository.
CAP Developer Guide: Role Assignment with AMS
AMS documentation

entry.title: Building a SaaS Multitenant Application Using SAP CAP Framework — Without a Database
entry.content:
If you have worked with SAP BTP before, you are likely already familiar with the basics of SAP Cloud Application Programming Model (CAP) development, at least within a Trial Account. Rather than starting from the very beginning, this blog assumes a foundational understanding of CAP and focuses on a specific, practical goal: building a SaaS (Software as a Service) multitenant application using the SAP CAP framework, without any database module.

The result is a clean, lightweight SaaS application that demonstrates tenant isolation, subscription lifecycle management, and secure routing — all without the complexity of database provisioning.

What is Multitenancy?

Before we dive in, here is a quick refresher on the concept at the heart of this blog: -

Multitenancy is a software architecture in which a single instance of an application serves multiple customers, known as tenants. Each tenant is logically separated and operates as if they have their own isolated environment, even though they share the same underlying resources — such as the application, infrastructure, and (optionally) databases. 

In the context of SAP BTP, the provider is the organization that develops and deploys the application, while the consumers are the subscriber subaccounts that use it. The SaaS Registry service manages this relationship.

Architecture Overview

Our application consists of the following components:

Component

Purpose

Approuter

Handles tenant isolation, authentication (XSUAA), and request routing

CAP Service (srv)

Exposes OData services and handles SaaS Registry subscription callbacks

XSUAA

Provides OAuth2 security with shared tenant mode for multi-tenant JWT handling

SaaS Registry

Manages tenant subscriptions — onboarding and offboarding lifecycle

Project Setup

Step 1: Initialize the Project

Start by creating a new CAP project and adding the required modules. Run the following commands in your terminal:

# Create a new CAP project
cds init cap-saas-app
cds add xsuaa approuter
cds add mta
npm install

Since this application does not use a database, remove the db/ folder from your project. It is not needed and will keep your project clean.

Configuring the Service and Approuter

Add one property to the service module - "APPROUTER_FQDN"

properties:
      APPROUTER_FQDN: "${org}-${space}-cap-saas-approuter.${default-domain}"

The Approuter is the entry point for all tenant requests. It is responsible for identifying which tenant is making the request, authenticating the user via XSUAA, and forwarding the request to the correct backend service.

TENANT_HOST_PATTERN

The most important configuration for multitenancy is  TENANT_HOST_PATTERN . This is a regular expression that the Approuter uses to extract the tenant subdomain from the incoming request hostname.

# In mta.yaml — under the approuter module properties:
TENANT_HOST_PATTERN: "^(.*)-${org}-${space}-${app-name}.${default-domain}"
SAP_JWT_TRUST_ACL:
  - clientid: "*"
    identityzone: "sap-provisioning"

The MTA placeholders resolve at deploy time. For example, in a Trial account with org  7ce04bc6trial  and space  dev , the approuter FQDN (Fully Qualified Domain Name) becomes:

7ce04bc6trial-dev-cap-saas-approuter.cfapps.us10-001.hana.ondemand.com

When a consumer with subdomain  consumer accesses the application, the full URL becomes:

https://consumer-7ce04bc6trial-dev-cap-saas-approuter.cfapps.us10-001.hana.ondemand.com

The Approuter applies  TENANT_HOST_PATTERN  to extract  consumer as the tenant subdomain, which XSUAA then uses to resolve the correct identity zone.

SaaS Registry Configuration

The SaaS Registry service is the backbone of subscription management. It is defined as a managed service resource in  mta.yaml  and is responsible for calling your application’s callback endpoints whenever a tenant subscribes or unsubscribes.

resources:
  - name: cap-saas-app-registry
    type: org.cloudfoundry.managed-service
    requires:
      - name: srv-api
    parameters:
      service: saas-registry
      service-plan: application
      config:
        xsappname: cap-saas-app-xsuaa
        appName: cap-saas
        displayName: 'CAP Multitenant Application'
        description: 'A SaaS app built with SAP CAP'
        category: "Partner Solutions"
        appUrls:
          onSubscription: ~{srv-api/srv-url}/callback/v1.0/tenants/{tenantId}
          onSubscriptionAsync: false
          onUnSubscription: ~{srv-api/srv-url}/callback/v1.0/tenants/{tenantId}
          onUnSubscriptionAsync: false
          getDependencies: ~{srv-api/srv-url}/callback/v1.0/dependencies

The appUrls reference the CAP service (srv) URL — not the Approuter. The SaaS Registry calls these endpoints directly during subscription events, bypassing the Approuter.

Security Configuration (xs-security.json)

The  xs-security.json  file configures your XSUAA instance. For a multitenant application, two settings are critical:

  • tenant-mode: shared — allows the XSUAA instance to serve multiple tenants from a single binding.
  • redirect-uris — must cover all possible Approuter URLs across CF regions
{
  "xsappname": "cap-saas-app-xsuaa",
  "tenant-mode": "shared",
  "scopes": [
    {
      "name": "$XSAPPNAME.Callback",
      "description": "Subscription via SaaS Registry",
      "grant-as-authority-to-apps": [
        "$XSAPPNAME(application,sap-provisioning,tenant-onboarding)"
      ]
    }
  ],
  "oauth2-configuration": {
    "redirect-uris": [
      "https://*.cfapps.us10-001.hana.ondemand.com/login/callback",
      "https://*.cfapps.us10.hana.ondemand.com/login/callback"
    ],
    "credential-types": ["binding-secret", "x509"]
  }
}

Subscription Callbacks (server.js)

This is where the magic happens. SAP CAP allows you to extend its bootstrap process via  cds.on("bootstrap", ...) . We use this hook to register the raw Express.js routes that the SaaS Registry will call during tenant subscription and unsubscription events.

The bootstrap event is fired immediately after the Express.js app is created, before any CAP middleware or services are added. This makes it the ideal place to register raw REST callbacks.  More info: https://cap.cloud.sap/docs/node.js/cds-server#built-in-server-js

Subscribe Callback (PUT)

When a consumer subscribes, the SaaS Registry calls  PUT /callback/v1.0/tenants/:tenantId . Your handler must return the tenant-specific Approuter URL as a plain string. This URL is stored by the SaaS Registry and used to redirect the consumer.

srv/server.js

"use strict";
const cds = require("@sap/cds");
const express = require("express");
cds.on("bootstrap", (app) => {
  app.use(express.json());
  // Called by SaaS Registry when a tenant subscribes
  app.put("/callback/v1.0/tenants/:tenantId", async (req, res) => {
    const { tenantId } = req.params;
    const body = req.body || {};
    const subscribedSubdomain = body.subscribedSubdomain || tenantId;
    const approuterFQDN = (process.env.APPROUTER_FQDN || "")
      .replace(/^https?:\/\//i, "")
      .replace(/\/+$/, "");
    const tenantUrl = `https://${subscribedSubdomain}-${approuterFQDN}`;
    res.status(200).send(tenantUrl);
  });
  // Called by SaaS Registry when a tenant unsubscribes
  app.delete("/callback/v1.0/tenants/:tenantId", async (req, res) => {
    const { tenantId } = req.params;
    res.status(200).send(`Tenant ${tenantId} successfully unsubscribed.`);
  });
});
module.exports = cds.server;

Important: The subscribe callback must return the APPROUTER URL — not the srv (service) URL. Returning the srv URL will cause XSUAA authentication to fail, as the srv app has no login handler.

Defining the CAP Service

Now for the application logic. We define a simple service that returns tenant-aware information to the consumer. CAP automatically populates  req.tenant ,  req.user , and  req.locale  from the validated XSUAA JWT — no manual token parsing required.

info-service.cds — Service Definition

The CDS file name and the service implementation file name must match for CAP to auto-wire them. We name both  info-service .

// srv/info-service.cds
@requires: 'authenticated-user'
service InfoService {
    function getSubaccount() returns String;
}

info-service.js — Service Implementation

// srv/info-service.js
const cds = require('@sap/cds');
module.exports = cds.service.impl(async function () {
    this.on('getSubaccount', async (req) => {
        // CAP auto-populates these from the XSUAA JWT
        const tenant = req.tenant  || 'unknown';
        const user   = req.user?.id || 'anonymous';
        const locale = req.locale   || 'en';
        return `Tenant: ${tenant} | User: ${user} | Locale: ${locale}`;
    });
});

CAP Auto-Wiring Convention: The file srv/info-service.js is automatically linked to the service defined in srv/info-service.cds because they share the same filename. If the names do not match, CAP cannot wire them and you will get a 501 “No handler” error at runtime.

Deployment

Step 1: Build and Deploy

# Build the MTA archive
mbt build
# Deploy to Cloud Foundry
cf deploy mta_archives/cap-saas-app_1.0.0.mtar

After a successful deployment, two applications will be running in your BTP space:

  • cap-saas-srv — the CAP backend service.
  • cap-saas-approuter — the tenant-aware entry point

Step 2: Subscribe from a Consumer Subaccount

Navigate to your consumer subaccount in the BTP Cockpit and subscribe to CAP Multitenant Application from the Service Marketplace. The SaaS Registry will call your  PUT /callback/v1.0/tenants/:tenantId  endpoint and store the returned tenant URL.

Step 3: Map the Tenant Route

On BTP Trial, wildcard routes are not permitted. After each subscription, you must manually map a CF route for the consumer tenant:

cf map-route cap-saas-approuter cfapps.us10-001.hana.ondemand.com \
  --hostname consumer-7ce04bc6trial-dev-cap-saas-approuter

Replace 'consumer' with the actual subdomain of your consumer subaccount. Run 'cf routes' to verify the route was created successfully.

Testing the Application

Once the route is mapped and the consumer has subscribed, open the tenant URL in a browser:

Consumer 2 -

The tenant ID corresponds to the consumer subaccount ID in BTP — confirming that full tenant isolation is working correctly.

Conclusion

In this blog, we built a fully functional SaaS multitenant application on SAP BTP using the CAP framework — with no database required. Here is a summary of what we covered:

  1. Initialized a CAP project with XSUAA and Approuter using cds add commands
  2. Configured TENANT_HOST_PATTERN to extract tenant subdomains from incoming request hostnames
  3. Set up the SaaS Registry with subscription callback URLs in mta.yaml
  4. Configured xs-security.json with tenant-mode: shared and correct redirect URIs
  5. Implemented PUT and DELETE subscription callbacks in server.js using the CAP bootstrap hook
  6. Defined a tenant-aware CAP service using the auto-wiring filename convention
  7. Deployed to Cloud Foundry and mapped tenant routes manually for BTP Trial
